breaking_news.title: Stryker Hit by Iran-Linked Wiper Attack Disrupting Global Operations
breaking_news.summary:
Medical technology giant Stryker confirmed a major cyberattack on March 11, 2026, attributed to the Iran-linked hacktivist group Handala. The group claimed to have deployed wiper malware that erased data from over 200,000 systems—including servers, mobile devices, and endpoints—across 79 countries, forcing global office shutdowns and halting critical medical device operations. The attack occurred amid heightened geopolitical tensions following U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran.

The impact is severe: Stryker’s global operations were crippled, with employees reporting devices wiped and systems offline. As a critical supplier of medical devices (including implants and surgical equipment), the attack posed direct risks to patient care and hospital workflows. The incident highlights the growing use of destructive wiper malware by state-aligned actors to inflict operational disruption beyond data theft.

breaking_news.title: CISA Adds Ivanti EPM, Cisco SD-WAN, and SolarWinds Flaws to KEV Catalog Amid Active Exploitation
breaking_news.summary:
CISA added three critical vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog on March 11, 2026: CVE-2026-1603 in Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM), CVE-2025-26399 in SolarWinds Web Help Desk, and CVE-2021-22054 in Omnissa Workspace ONE. These flaws—patched in February 2026—are now being actively exploited in the wild, enabling unauthenticated credential theft, SSRF, and deserialization attacks. Ivanti EPM’s CVE-2026-1603 alone allows remote attackers to leak stored credentials.

The KEV designation triggers mandatory patching deadlines for U.S. federal agencies and pressures enterprise defenders to prioritize remediation. Given the widespread use of these products in critical infrastructure and enterprise environments, unpatched systems remain highly vulnerable to ransomware and espionage campaigns by APT groups.

breaking_news.title: PhantomRaven Supply Chain Attack Compromises 88 npm Packages to Steal Developer Data
breaking_news.summary:
A new wave of the PhantomRaven supply chain campaign has compromised 88 malicious npm packages targeting JavaScript developers. The packages, disguised as legitimate tools, exfiltrate sensitive data—including API keys, environment variables, and source code—via obfuscated JavaScript. Attackers leverage social engineering and fake documentation to trick developers into installing the packages, often through CI/CD pipelines or local development environments.

The implications are severe: compromised developer machines can lead to downstream compromise of production applications, container images, and cloud deployments. With npm being a central dependency repository, this campaign poses a high risk of cascading impact across enterprises using open-source tooling, especially those with automated build systems.

breaking_news.title: BlackSanta Malware Targets HR with EDR-Killing ISO Attachments
breaking_news.summary:
A Russian-speaking threat actor is distributing resumés via phishing emails containing malicious ISO files that deploy the BlackSanta malware. Once executed, BlackSanta disables antivirus and EDR protections at the kernel level before installing credential harvesters, info-stealers, and remote access tools. The campaign specifically targets HR departments, exploiting trust in recruitment workflows and bypassing email filters via cloud-hosted ISOs.

This attack demonstrates a shift toward infrastructure abuse (legitimate cloud storage), EDR evasion, and targeted social engineering. By disabling endpoint defenses first, attackers ensure long-term persistence and successful data exfiltration—posing serious insider threat and credential compromise risks to affected organizations.

breaking_news.title: APT28 Deploys BEARDSHELL and COVENANT Malware in Long-Term Espionage Against Ukrainian Forces
breaking_news.summary:
APT28 (Fancy Bear) has been conducting persistent espionage against Ukrainian military personnel since April 2024 using two custom implants: BEARDSHELL and COVENANT. These tools, with code lineage tracing back to the group’s 2010-era malware, enable long-term surveillance, data exfiltration, and command-and-control via dual-cloud infrastructure for resilience. ESET’s analysis confirms the group’s continued evolution and operational tempo despite international sanctions and law enforcement actions.

The campaign underscores APT28’s strategic focus on Ukrainian defense and government targets amid ongoing conflict. The use of custom, dual-implant architecture reflects sophisticated tradecraft and highlights the enduring threat posed by Russian state-sponsored actors to NATO-aligned and conflict-zone forces.

breaking_news.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Modified AuraInspector to Mass-Scan Salesforce for Data Theft
breaking_news.summary:
Threat actors are mass-scanning Salesforce Experience Cloud sites using a modified version of the open-source AuraInspector tool to exploit overly permissive guest user configurations. Salesforce confirmed the campaign, which enables attackers to access sensitive records—including Accounts, Contacts, and Leads—without authentication. ShinyHunters has claimed responsibility, stating they have compromised hundreds of organizations, including Salesforce itself.

The attack leverages a legitimate tool in an unauthorized way, bypassing detection and enabling large-scale data harvesting. With Salesforce widely used for CRM and customer data, this campaign poses significant compliance (GDPR, HIPAA) and reputational risks to affected enterprises.

breaking_news.title: KadNap Botnet Hijacks 14,000+ ASUS Routers to Route Malicious Traffic
breaking_news.summary:
The KadNap malware has infected over 14,000 edge devices—primarily ASUS routers—turning them into a stealth proxy botnet. First detected in August 2025, the campaign uses a peer-to-peer Kademlia-based C2 infrastructure to route malicious traffic through a proxy named Doppelganger (likely linked to TheMoon malware). The U.S. accounts for over 60% of infections, with victims across Europe and Asia also affected.

This infrastructure abuse enables attackers to anonymize phishing, ransomware, and APT operations while evading IP-based blocklists. Compromised routers act as trusted relays, undermining network perimeter security and complicating threat hunting for defenders.

breaking_news.title: Bell Ambulance Breach Exposes 238,000 Patients to Medusa Ransomware Leak
breaking_news.summary:
Bell Ambulance, a U.S.-based emergency medical services provider, confirmed a February 2025 breach affecting 238,000 individuals after the Medusa ransomware group claimed responsibility and leaked over 219 GB of stolen data. The breach exposed highly sensitive information including Social Security numbers, driver’s license numbers, financial data, and full medical records. Forensic investigation confirmed unauthorized access on February 13, 2025, with disclosure delayed until April 14, 2026.

As a critical healthcare provider, Bell Ambulance’s breach poses severe identity theft and fraud risks to patients. The delay in disclosure and scale of data exfiltration highlight vulnerabilities in third-party vendor risk management and incident response timelines in the healthcare sector.
